title: Jacques Villeneuve suspects Lewis Hamilton got in Charles Leclerc's head at Canadian GP
content:
Jacques Villeneuve suspects that Lewis Hamilton got into Charles Leclerc’s head during the Canadian Grand Prix.
On Sunday, Kimi Antonelli extended his lead at the top of the standings to 43 points after George Russell failed to finish the Canadian Grand Prix.
What Kanye thinks about Lewis Hamilton and Kim Kardashian's romance — and why he really 'hated' Pete Davidson
Russell and Antonelli were battling for the lead before a power unit issue halted the former’s progress, forcing him to retire on Lap 30.
From there,
Antonelli won the race
ahead of Ferrari’s Hamilton and Red Bull star Max Verstappen.
Meanwhile, Leclerc, who was frustrated with his own performance all weekend, finished in fourth place.
Is Charles Leclerc good enough to win multiple F1 world championships?
Photo by Alessio Morgese/NurPhoto via Getty Images
tyler.rowlinson
Featured comment
If this was a couple of years ago, I'd have said yes, no doubt. But while he has immense talent, I'm not sure he's got it in him now to win a title, let alone multiple. I think his time has passed
aaditya.krishnamurthy
Featured comment
He definitely has the talent, but his time is running out. Luck has never been on his side, so he needs to make his luck if that has to happen
david.comerford
Featured comment
He'll probably never be in the GOAT conversation don't get me wrong, but I'm pretty confident Leclerc could win more than one title with a better car
Jacques Villeneuve suspects Lewis Hamilton got in Charles Leclerc’s head in Canada
Speaking after the race, 1997 Formula 1 world champion Villeneuve questioned
Leclerc
’s team radio message. The Canadian also stated that Leclerc’s frustration was clear for everyone to see.
Villeneuve
said on
F1TV
: “You can see him frustrated, not happy. He tells the radio, ‘Don’t tell me what lap time Lewis is doing, I’m not interested, it’s not important’.
“Actually, it is an important thing. You need to know what your teammate is [doing], you need communication. You can see the frustration and his mistakes on the restart. That was so close.
“It was good to see Lewis be able to drive aggressively. The car seems to be very precise. He was sliding it, but not destroying the tyres. He was having fun.”
Should Charles Leclerc be worried about Lewis Hamilton’s upturn in form?
aaditya.krishnamurthy
Featured comment
I’d say yes, but with Monaco this week? I can’t rule out against Leclerc hunting for the win
david.comerford
Featured comment
There's definitely a tendency to overreact to Hamilton's good weekends. Let's remember that Charles was a long way clear in Miami on pace alone before a mistake late on
tyler.rowlinson
Featured comment
Hamilton is really turning a corner at Ferrari. If I was Leclerc, there is still time to recover, but I would definitely be worried
READ MORE:
Martin Brundle says Charles Leclerc still lacks one attribute that all F1 greats have
Charles Leclerc reflects on ‘incredibly difficult’ Canadian Grand Prix
Speaking after the race, Leclerc claimed that he had ‘zero feeling’ with the tyres from the very first lap in FP1.
The Monegasque also admitted that it was ‘probably’ the ‘most difficult’ weekend of his F1 career to date.
As per
RacingNews365
, Leclerc said: “It has been a nightmare weekend for me, probably the most difficult weekend of my F1 career.
“I have zero feeling with the tyres from the first lap of FP1 until the very last lap of the race, and in the last 15 laps, I was driving a second-and-a-half off the pace just not to take the risk.
“Even in these laps, I still had moments that were too close for comfort, and it has been an incredibly difficult weekend.”

title: Lance Stroll told Aston Martin he finished Canadian GP 'for the mechanics and Lawrence'
content:
Aston Martin driver Lance Stroll sounded extremely frustrated over the radio during Sunday’s Canadian Grand Prix.
Stroll
finished the race 15th, and while that was his best result of the season so far, he was still four laps down on the leaders.
10 Notable Cars Featuring UK-Built V8 Engines
Even more damning, he was also lapped by Esteban Ocon, the driver in 14th. He only beat the Cadillac of Valtteri Bottas, with retirements promoting him up the order.
Has Lawrence Stroll made too many changes at Aston Martin?
Photos by Kym Illman/Gold & Goose Photography/Getty Images
fogclimber1998
Featured comment
Lawrence Stroll is like the Congress in the USA. Just throw lots of money at it and the problems will disappear.  It never works. He has built a great facility for the team, but other decisions he has made are poor at best. He did not back up Andy Cowell in the disagreement with Adrian Newey and he gave Newey too much power. Honda is another mistake.
1
aaditya.krishnamurthy
Featured comment
Steiner is bang on here, Stroll has to realise there is no overnight success in Formula 1
david.comerford
Featured comment
Look how long it took Andrea Stella at McLaren, he's let some good people go and moved them around too much
‘I hope the coffee’s really good’ – Lance Stroll’s remarkable Canadian Grand Prix radio
Stroll knows that he has no realistic chance of points at this stage of the season given the extensively documented issues with Aston Martin’s car. The
Honda engine is severely underpowered
, so much so that rival manufacturers have agreed to let them exceed the cost cap, and the chassis is also lacking.
Still, one can understand why Stroll found the experience of being relentlessly lapped at his home race painful.
In a radio message at the beginning of the race, he told the team: “I’m f—— slow.”
The 27-year-old was also critical of the team for the lack of proactive assistance, though they were struggling to hear him.
“It’s like you’re sleeping… just leaving me out here,” he remarked at one stage. “I hope the coffee’s really good on the pit wall.”
What have you made of Lance Stroll’s attitude at Aston Martin this season?
Photo by Kym Illman/Getty Images
Later, he complained: “The amazing thing is, with all the locking in the last five laps, you guys haven’t recommended one switch change.”
Stroll told engineer Stephen Glass that he only carried on racing out of respect for the mechanics and his father Lawrence, who owns the team.
“I’m completing the race for the mechanics, that’s it,” he said. “And Lawrence. I’m completing the race for the mechanics and Lawrence.”
Stroll’s teammate Fernando Alonso retired from the Canadian GP
due to a seat issue that left him nursing back pain. The Spaniard may have continued had points been on the cards.
Alonso says he finally has ‘hope’ for the upcoming races
, even if Aston Martin’s first major upgrades won’t arrive until the late summer. While he qualified 19th, he was only three-tenths slower than the Williams of Alex Albon in qualifying.
Stroll lined up 21st between the two Cadillac drivers, a second off the pace of his teammate.

title: McLaren's 'disaster' strategy department has been exposed now they don't have 'a dominant car'
content:
McLaren’s strategists have been under fire following the Canadian Grand Prix, after they fielded Lando Norris and Oscar Piastri on wet-weather tyres despite the majority of the grid running slicks.
The Australian driver raised concerns over the tyre choice during the three formation laps that took place ahead of lights out. Despite the ample amount of time that they had to rectify the choice,
Andrea Stella
and co stuck to their guns.
Audi F1 – Gabriel Bortoleto visits Ingolstadt
Why do McLaren keep making strategy errors?
Photo by James Sutton – Formula 1/Formula 1 via Getty Images
aaditya.krishnamurthy
Featured comment
I think McLaren often have this paranoia of getting the deicision or strategy wrong, which ironically, ends up resulting in them getting their strategy wrong by overthinking things
fogclimber1998
Featured comment
Where on earth were they looking? That track was not suited for intermediate tires. Who decided to go with them???
jonathanmaze
Featured comment
Someone in that team doesn’t have a clue
1
As the five lights dropped, it looked like it could have been somewhat of a masterstroke.
Norris
immediately shot himself up to P1 off the line, but it quickly became apparent that the intermediate weather tyres were not the right compound to be on.
Once the rest of the grid got their slick tyres up to temperature, Kimi Antonelli was quickly on Norris’ rear wing. The intermediates began to run red-hot, and both Norris and
Piastri
were called in for a set of medium-compound tyres before the end of lap three.
Christijan Albers blasts McLaren’s ‘disaster’ of a race strategy at the Canadian GP
Speaking via
De Telegraaf’s F1 podcast
, former driver
Christijan Albers
blasted
McLaren’s
race strategists, likening them to Ferrari’s questionable tactics in recent years.
The Dutch pundit began by noting that the fact that McLaren no longer have a car that can get away with utilising the wrong strategy, and so wrong decisions on the pit wall no longer have a much bigger effect on their results at the chequered flag.
Even Aston Martin have had better reliability than McLaren this year. Are the world champions already out of contention for the constructors’ title?
Photos by Mark Thompson/Kym Illman/Getty Images
aaditya.krishnamurthy
Featured comment
Have we forgotten what happened 2 years ago? There’s a lot of racing left to be had, and McLaren can close the gap
marcus.chan
Featured comment
Too early to say they are out, but they need to step up their game if they want to keep Mercedes within touching distance!
tyler.rowlinson
Featured comment
McLaren's title hopes are done. I think they'll improve as the season goes on, but no way do they catch Mercedes
“A dominant car doesn’t mean the team is just spotless or functioning superbly,” he said. “We always talked about that last year, on the strategy side, it’s just a real disaster with those guys. Here, you saw it again; it was a combination of both.
“You’ve got two drivers who aren’t pushing them enough. Piastri does mention it on the radio, but that is only so he can say afterwards, ‘Yeah, I did bring it up.’ But it doesn’t get through, and he drives into the pit. Norris was also unsure.
“They’ve lost positions to Mercedes because they just aren’t on top of it. They aren’t being aggressive, and it’s just really sloppy. Don’t forget, we talked about this two, three, four years ago, when Ferrari was simply written off completely.”
As a result, McLaren suffered their first scoreless finish with at least one car crossing the line since the 2023 Canadian Grand Prix.
Of course, a swathe of other issues hindered their race, too. Piastri’s overly ambitious move on Alex Albon at turn 10 saw him slapped with a 10-second penalty, and a reliability issue on Norris’ MCL40 saw him enter the pits one too many times before he was forced to retire.

title: George Russell looked like ‘a ghost’ in the F1 paddock after Canadian Grand Prix retirement
content:
George Russell was a shell of his usual self after retiring from the Canadian Grand Prix, as the Brit looked like a ‘ghost’ in the paddock.
Russell
was looking to bounce back and stamp his authority on Kimi Antonelli in Montreal. The 28-year-old lost to his younger teammate in the previous three races, and the
Canadian Grand Prix
is typically one of his strongest tracks.
T-Mobile CEO on New Plan to Simplify Switching Carriers
After winning the Sprint, Russell had an intense battle with his teammate in the first half of the race. The pair fought tirelessly for the lead, running off the track and locking up numerous times, before the No.63 driver suddenly ground to a halt on lap 30.
READ MORE:
Mercedes driver George Russell’s life outside F1 from net worth to height
Photo by Kym Illman/Getty Images
George Russell looked like a ‘ghost’ after retiring from the Canadian Grand Prix
Russell suffered a battery failure
as he entered turn nine, forcing him to stop on track and hand the lead to Antonelli, who went on to win his fourth race in a row.
Jacques Villeneuve says Antonelli was ‘lucky’
due to the mistakes he made throughout the race. But ultimately, he extends his lead in the championship to 43 points, and his teammate now has an even bigger mountain to climb.
Russell was inconsolable after the race, as he was seen throwing his headrest onto the track and throwing his gloves off in anger on the sidelines.
Is George Russell right to apologise for throwing his headrest after his Canadian GP retirement?
aaditya.krishnamurthy
Featured comment
It was definitely not a good look, not just for someone who is in the title race, but also as an F1 driver, you just can’t act the way he did without some sort of punitive damage, given how strict they are about these things
gramorris
Featured comment
Not a great idea is it but if it was a problem the punishment is hardly a deterrent. Bit of a waste of time all in all
callum.boyle65
Featured comment
It’s a heat in the moment thing that has been blown way out of proportion. We’ve all lost our cool after a frustrating incident. Nobody was hurt and that’s the main thing
According to a report from
Autonocion
, he was ‘on the verge of tears’ as marshals came to collect his stricken Mercedes from the side of the track. In the paddock, he ‘wandered around like a ghost’ with his head down.
F1 fans have a new nickname for Russell
, likening him to Anakin Skywalker after seeing his anger after his retirement. Many believe that he is heading to the ‘dark side’, and his demeanour in the paddock makes it hard to argue otherwise.
‘Tensions’ are brewing at Mercedes
as Russell and Antonelli did not look at each other after their battle on track in Canada.
The Brit is clearly frustrated with his situation, and a lot of it has been no fault of his own. He will need to bounce back soon, but most importantly, he has to keep a level head.

title: La Ferrari si gode il nuovo Hamilton: "Sono riuscito ad attaccare in ogni curva" | Gazzetta.it
content:
L'ottimo secondo posto in Canada ha ridato fiducia all'inglese: "Non immaginate quanto lavoro ci sia stato dietro. Il simulatore? Voglio aiutare i tecnici per usarlo al meglio". Sul rapporto con l'ingegnere Carlo Santi: "È fantastico, mi piace lavorare con lui"
Jacopo Moretti
26 maggio - 17:28
- MILANO
“Mi sono divertito così tanto. La macchina era fantastica in ogni giro”. Che il podio conquistato da Lewis Hamilton in Canada avesse un valore speciale lo si era capito fin da subito, quando il britannico, via radio, si era rivolto al suo team in italiano: “Grazie a tutti! Questo è il risultato che ci meritiamo!”. E in effetti il ferrarista è sembrato trovarsi finalmente a suo agio con la SF-26, al punto da portarsi a sole tre lunghezze dal compagno di box Leclerc in classifica, con fiducia e numeri ben diversi da quelli dello scorso anno. Ma cos’è cambiato?
preparazione
—
Era da tempo che non si vedeva un Hamilton così, aggressivo in pista – splendido il duello che l’ha visto prevalere su Verstappen per il secondo posto – ed entusiasta una volta sceso dalla sua SF-26, con tanto di abbracci e stretta di mano con lo storico rivale olandese. “Non so nemmeno spiegarvi quanto ho dovuto lavorare duramente per arrivare a questo punto”, racconta lui. Eppure, una spiegazione c’è: già prima del GP Lewis aveva rivelato di non trovarsi a suo agio con il simulatore del Cavallino, tanto da decidere di non utilizzarlo nella preparazione del weekend canadese. Una scelta già intrapresa anche in Cina, dove, non a caso, ha ottenuto l’unico altro podio di questa stagione. “Sono un pilota vecchia scuola. I miei due migliori risultati quest’anno sono arrivati senza simulatore e in tutti i campionati precedenti, escluso forse il 2008, non l’ho mai usato”. E pare proprio che Hamilton si sia convinto a non utilizzare la “pista virtuale” di Maranello nemmeno per i prossimi GP: “Non lo farò, è troppo rischioso e me la cavo meglio senza”. Ma guai a pensare che tra Lewis e il simulatore sia una storia chiusa: “Voglio aiutare la squadra a capire quali sono i problemi”, spiega piuttosto. “So che i collaudatori dicono che tutto funziona correttamente, ma siamo soltanto io e Charles a poterlo dire davvero, siamo noi a salire sulla macchina”.
con santi ok
—
E in attesa di risolvere le difficoltà di correlazione con la fabbrica, Hamilton si gode il suo miglior risultato (in gara) da quando è in Ferrari, dedicando uno speciale ringraziamento a Carlo Santi, che dall’Australia lo accompagna come nuovo ingegnere di pista: “È assolutamente fantastico, mi piace molto lavorare con lui”. E per quanto la presenza di Santi “in cuffia” fosse destinata ad essere soltanto temporanea, pare che a Maranello non ci sia grande fretta di promuovere il nuovo arrivato dalla McLaren Cedric Michel-Grosjean, comunque celebrato da Hamilton: “Mi ha aiutato molto ad ottenere più prestazione della vettura, abbiamo apportato delle modifiche al setup e sono riuscito ad attaccare tutte le curve come volevo”.
conferme
—
Ma non è soltanto il tandem Santi – Grosjean a funzionare, dato che Hamilton sembra aver trovato nella SF-26 una vettura finalmente adatta al suo stile di guida: “La macchina andava alla grande, sentivo di riuscire a spingere ad ogni giro, e poterlo fare su un tracciato come Montreal è stato semplicemente eccezionale”. Merito (anche) del team principal Vasseur, che ha sempre insistito su come l’inglese necessitasse di tempo per portare la sua mentalità vincente in squadra: “Fred mi ha sostenuto moltissimo e ancora una volta ha fatto di tutto per farmi sentire a mio agio. Ha davvero mosso le montagne per me, e questo sta iniziando a riflettersi nelle mie prestazioni”. Che Hamilton abbia ritrovato se stesso? Presto per dirlo. Cina e Canada sono da sempre piste particolarmente gradite all’inglese, chiamato ora a confermarsi con l’inizio della stagione europea. E già a Monaco, dove sono aerodinamica e qualità meccaniche a contare più del motore, Ferrari promette di poter fare la voce grossa. Sarà quella di Lewis?
